Staging tests are slow, sequential, and require real cloud resources; Molecule runs in seconds in Docker, catches idempotency and syntax errors early, and avoids contaminating shared infrastructure with a broken role.

followups.q: How do you share Molecule test infrastructure across many roles in a monorepo?
followups.a: Define a shared `molecule.yml` template and symlink or inherit it via Molecule's `extends` configuration. Centralise the driver and platform definitions so each role only overrides its scenario-specific settings. In CI, run `molecule test` in parallel across roles using a matrix job. A shared `requirements.yml` in the repo root installs common role dependencies once.

q: When would you run Terraform and Ansible together in the same pipeline, and how do you hand off between them safely?
a: A common two-stage pipeline runs Terraform to provision the infrastructure (VMs, networking, security groups) and then Ansible to configure the freshly-created instances. The handoff requires Terraform to emit the instance IPs or DNS names as outputs, which the pipeline captures (e.g. `terraform output -json`) and converts into an Ansible inventory. Safe handoff practices: wait for instance SSH readiness (not just API existence) using Ansible's `wait_for_connection` before converging, pass Terraform outputs as extra-vars or a generated inventory file, and ensure the Ansible step runs in the same pipeline job so the ordering is enforced, not assumed. If Terraform provisions AMI baked by a Packer+Ansible run, the runtime Ansible pass is thinner, following an immutable-infra pattern.
fs: Capture Terraform outputs as JSON, generate Ansible inventory from them, use wait_for_connection before converging. Baked AMIs (Packer+Ansible) reduce runtime Ansible surface.
fj: I just hardcode the IPs in the Ansible inventory and run Ansible separately after Terraform finishes.

followups.q: What does wait_for_connection do and why is it essential in a Terraform+Ansible pipeline?
followups.a: `wait_for_connection` polls the target host until SSH is reachable and Python is available, retrying for a configurable timeout. Terraform's apply returns as soon as the cloud API reports the instance 'running', but cloud-init, network plumbing, and SSHD startup can take 30–120 seconds more. Without this gate, the first Ansible task may fail with a connection timeout and leave the host partially configured.

followups.q: How does the immutable infrastructure pattern with Packer reduce the Ansible runtime blast radius?
followups.a: In the immutable pattern, Packer builds a machine image using an Ansible provisioner, baking all packages, config, and secrets into the AMI. Terraform then provisions instances from that image with no further Ansible configuration pass at boot. If something is wrong, you rebuild the image and rotate instances. This eliminates the SSH-reachability race, agent-bootstrap complexity, and runtime config drift, at the cost of longer build pipelines.

followups.q: How do you handle Terraform-provisioned resources that require Ansible re-convergence on drift, not just initial setup?
followups.a: Schedule periodic Ansible playbook runs against dynamic inventory (via AWX, Ansible Automation Platform, or a CI cron job) that re-converge the running fleet. Terraform manages resource lifecycle changes; Ansible handles in-place config drift. Alerting on large `changed` counts in scheduled runs signals unexpected drift. This continuous convergence loop is what replaces the self-healing of agent-based tools.

q: How do Ansible and Terraform handle configuration drift differently, and what is the right remediation strategy for each?
a: Terraform detects drift by comparing live resource attributes to its state file during the plan refresh phase (`terraform plan -refresh-only`). It reports what changed out-of-band but does not automatically fix it; you decide whether to accept the drift into state or re-apply config to revert it. Ansible detects drift differently: re-running an idempotent playbook against live hosts produces 'changed' for any host that deviated, and the playbook immediately converges those hosts back to the desired state. The two tools thus offer complementary remediation: Terraform surfaces and controls cloud-resource lifecycle drift deliberately; Ansible continuously repairs OS and application-layer drift on a schedule. A mature IaC stack uses Ansible Automation Platform or AWX to run Ansible playbooks on a cron and alert on high changed-task rates as a drift signal.
fs: Terraform surfaces cloud-layer drift in plan and lets you decide; Ansible automatically converges OS/app drift on each run. Both are needed for full-stack remediation.
fj: Drift only matters in Terraform; Ansible configs stay correct since we wrote them.

followups.q: Why can't Terraform auto-remediate drift the way Ansible can?
followups.a: Terraform's model requires a human or gated pipeline to review the plan and approve an apply, because cloud-resource changes can be destructive and irreversible. Auto-remediation would silently destroy and recreate resources to match config. Ansible tasks, by contrast, are OS-level writes with narrower blast radius and are designed for continuous re-application, so auto-convergence is safe and expected.

followups.q: How do you set up continuous Ansible drift remediation without a full AWX install?
followups.a: A simple pattern: a GitLab CI or GitHub Actions scheduled workflow runs `ansible-playbook -i inventory/ site.yml` on a timer (e.g. every hour), captures the return code and stdout, and posts a Slack alert if any host reports 'changed' or 'failed'. The job must use dynamic inventory so ephemeral cloud hosts are always current. This gives you continuous convergence with no additional infrastructure beyond your existing CI runner.

followups.q: When is accepting Terraform drift into state (apply -refresh-only) the right call?
followups.a: Accept it when the out-of-band change is intentional and authoritative, for example an ops team manually enabled a feature flag during an incident and the config should now reflect reality. Re-applying config would revert a deliberate operational decision. Document why you accepted it and follow up with a config change that encodes the new desired state, so the next plan shows no diff.

q: What is an Ansible playbook's structure at a senior level, and how does plays, roles, and tasks compose for a multi-tier deployment?
a: A playbook is a YAML list of plays. Each play targets one or more inventory groups, sets variables, applies privilege escalation, and lists tasks and/or roles to run against those hosts. For a multi-tier deployment you write separate plays: one for database servers, one for application servers, one for load balancers, each targeting its own inventory group. Roles are imported inside plays via `roles:` or `import_role` for static includes (graph-resolved at parse time) or `include_role` for dynamic includes (resolved at runtime, useful inside loops). Tags let you skip or target subsets of the playbook. Handlers defined in roles are scoped to their play, so a database handler won't fire when the app play runs, keeping blast radius contained.
fs: Multi-tier playbooks use one play per tier group. Prefer import_role for static graph inclusion; use include_role only when dynamic (loop/when) inclusion is required.
fj: I put all my tasks in one big play targeting all hosts and use when: statements to separate logic per host type.

followups.q: What's the functional difference between import_role and include_role, and when does it matter?
followups.a: `import_role` is static: Ansible resolves it at playbook parse time, so tags applied to the import propagate into every task in the role, and you can't use it inside a loop. `include_role` is dynamic: resolved at runtime, supports loops and conditional includes, but tags don't auto-propagate. Use `import_role` as the default for predictable tagging and graph visibility; switch to `include_role` only when you need runtime dynamism.

followups.q: How do you avoid variable collision when multiple roles set the same variable name?
followups.a: Role `defaults/` variables are namespaced by convention (e.g. `nginx_port`) but Ansible has no enforced namespacing. Strict convention is the primary safeguard: prefix all role variables with the role name. Also, use `vars/` for role-internal constants so callers can't accidentally override them, and audit role interfaces when composing roles from different authors in the same play.

followups.q: How do you run only the app tier of a multi-tier playbook without editing the file?
followups.a: Two approaches: pass `--limit app` to restrict host matching to just the `app` inventory group regardless of the `hosts:` value in each play; or tag every task in the app play/role with `tags: [app]` and run `ansible-playbook site.yml --tags app`. `--limit` is blunter and skips entire plays; `--tags` is more granular but requires tagging discipline in the roles.

q: How do you design a private Terraform module registry for a platform team serving many product teams?
a: A platform team's module registry serves versioned, opinionated building blocks that product teams consume without owning implementation. The key design axes are: (1) hosting choice — HCP Terraform Private Registry gives OIDC-integrated module publishing with semantic versioning out of the box; a self-hosted Gitea or Gitolite instance plus a thin registry API wrapper works for on-prem shops, as does Spacelift's module registry; (2) versioning discipline — every merge to main triggers a tagged release via semantic-release, and consumers pin to a minor range (e.g. ~> 2.3); (3) interface contracts — each module exposes a typed, minimal variable surface, publishes machine-readable outputs, and provides a module_docs.md generated from terraform-docs; (4) testing — every module has Terratest integration tests run in ephemeral cloud accounts in CI; (5) governance — deprecated modules are soft-archived with a 90-day notice period and a migration guide, not deleted. Avoid a mono-registry blob; group modules by domain (networking/, compute/, data-platform/) so team ownership maps cleanly.
fs: A platform registry needs versioned releases, typed minimal interfaces, auto-generated docs, Terratest CI, and domain-grouped module ownership — not a single blob everyone edits.
fj: A registry is just a Git repo with a docs folder; teams copy the module source directly.

code.label: HCP Terraform Private Registry source pin

followups.q: How do you enforce that product teams can't bypass the registry and use raw provider resources?
followups.a: Sentinel or OPA policies on the run pipeline can check that every resource of a certain type is owned by a permitted module address, rejecting configs that declare `aws_db_instance` outside the approved module namespace. Combined with IAM roles that scope the Terraform runner's permissions to what the module needs, you make the unapproved path both policy-blocked and permissions-blocked.

followups.q: What's the right deprecation workflow for a major module version bump that breaks callers?
followups.a: Publish the major bump (v4.0) alongside v3.x rather than replacing it. Announce the migration guide and a sunset date. Add a `lifecycle { precondition {} }` or README banner warning in v3 that fires a deprecation message in the plan. Run a platform-owned migration sprint tracking adoption via module_version telemetry. Hard-archive (remove from registry) only after all known callers migrate.

q: Walk through a workspace-vs-root-module state strategy for managing dev, staging, and prod at scale. When does each break down?
a: Workspaces (a single root module, multiple named state instances) are right when environments are structurally identical and share credentials — think ephemeral PR preview environments or load-test clones. They break down when environments need different provider credentials, IAM roles, blast-radius isolation, or differ enough in config that workspace-conditional logic (terraform.workspace == 'prod' ? ...) balloons. The scalable alternative is separate root modules per environment (often called the directory-per-env or stack-per-env model), each with its own backend key and assume-role ARN. This gives air-gapped credentials, clear blast radius, and independently plannable configs. A hybrid approach uses workspaces for ephemeral copies inside an environment and separate root configs for the durable tier boundaries. At very large orgs, Atlantis, Spacelift, or HCP Terraform stacks orchestrate cross-root dependency ordering without tight coupling.
fs: Workspaces = same credentials, identical shape, ephemeral. Separate root configs = per-env IAM, isolated blast radius, independent plans. Use both where appropriate.
fj: Use workspaces for everything; separating root configs is just unnecessary duplication.

followups.q: How does HCP Terraform Stacks change the calculus for multi-environment state management?
followups.a: Stacks (GA late 2024) introduce a first-class layer above workspaces: a stack definition deploys the same component configuration across multiple deployments (dev, staging, prod) while managing cross-deployment dependencies and ordering natively. This collapses the directory-per-env pattern into a single, declarative stack config with per-deployment variable overrides, combining workspace convenience with root-config isolation without manual orchestration glue.

followups.q: What state hygiene practices prevent workspace state files from accumulating indefinitely?
followups.a: Automate workspace lifecycle: on PR close, the pipeline runs `terraform destroy` in the ephemeral workspace then `terraform workspace delete` to remove the state file. Use backend lifecycle rules (S3 lifecycle policy on the state bucket prefix) to expire abandoned state objects after a retention window. Tag workspaces with PR number and owner so ownership is always traceable.

q: How do you implement policy-as-code for Terraform at an organization with hundreds of AWS accounts?
a: Policy-as-code for Terraform enforces organizational guardrails before any infrastructure changes land. The two primary engines are HashiCorp Sentinel (HCP Terraform/Enterprise only) and Open Policy Agent (OPA) via tools like Conftest. The workflow: (1) write policies in Sentinel/Rego that inspect the Terraform plan JSON (terraform show -json tfplan) for violations, e.g. no unencrypted S3 buckets, instance types are from an approved list, all resources are tagged with required keys; (2) gate policies in CI before apply — soft-mandatory (warning), mandatory (block), and advisory tiers so not everything halts the run; (3) distribute policies from a central policy repo, pinned versions, so product teams consume rather than write them; (4) complement with AWS Service Control Policies (SCPs) at the organization level, which enforce regardless of IaC tool, giving defense-in-depth. Policy tests live alongside policies in the same CI pipeline. At scale, dedicated policy modules cover domains (tagging, encryption, network egress) and teams override only where explicitly approved.
fs: Central Sentinel/OPA policies gate plan JSON before apply; SCPs add a cloud-side enforcement layer independent of any IaC tool. Tier policies by severity — not everything blocks.
fj: Policy checks are just README guidelines; developers read them and follow them.

followups.q: How do you write an OPA rule that blocks any S3 bucket without server-side encryption?
followups.a: Query the plan JSON's `resource_changes` array, filter for `aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration` resources being deleted or for `aws_s3_bucket` resources with no corresponding encryption config in the same plan. The rule emits a denial message for each violation. Pair it with a unit test using mock plan JSON that covers both pass and fail cases, run in CI alongside the policy itself.

followups.q: Why are SCPs not a replacement for Terraform policy-as-code, and vice versa?
followups.a: SCPs are evaluated by AWS IAM on every API call, so they enforce even if someone bypasses Terraform entirely (console click, AWS CLI). But they're binary allow/deny with no context about planned changes, can't give readable remediation messages, and lag behind plan by operating at apply time. Terraform policy-as-code operates pre-apply, provides human-readable guidance, and can catch configuration anti-patterns SCPs can't express. Both are needed for defense-in-depth.

q: What does a mature multi-account Terraform architecture look like, and how do you manage provider credential delegation across accounts?
a: A mature multi-account Terraform architecture uses separate AWS accounts per environment/team boundary (following the AWS Organizations model) with a dedicated 'management' or 'tooling' account that runs Terraform pipelines. Provider credential delegation works via IAM role assumption: the Terraform runner in the tooling account holds a base role that is allowed to `sts:AssumeRole` into target account roles (provisioner roles) with scoped permissions. Each root module configures an aliased provider block that assumes the target role at plan/apply time, giving full isolation and audit-trail clarity. All state lives in a centralized state account or S3 bucket cross-account-accessible only to the provisioner role. At the organization level this is codified in a 'landing zone' module (or AWS Control Tower) that provisions each new account with the IAM trust policy Terraform expects. Drift on the provisioner roles themselves is tracked by a dedicated account-management Terraform root that only the platform team applies.
fs: Tooling account runner assumes per-target provisioner roles via STS; landing zone modules bootstrap trust policies; state lives in a dedicated account with cross-account bucket policies.
fj: Each team manages their own AWS root credentials for Terraform; accounts are just organizational labels.

followups.q: How do you prevent a compromised tooling-account role from blasting prod?
followups.a: Scope each provisioner role with least-privilege IAM policies covering only what that root module provisions — a networking provisioner doesn't need EC2:* permissions. Add a session-policy condition that requires the OIDC token's `sub` matches the specific pipeline repo and branch (e.g. `ref:refs/heads/main`). Combine with SCPs that deny critical actions (e.g. account closure) even for assumed roles.

followups.q: How does AWS Control Tower / Landing Zone Accelerator interact with Terraform in a multi-account setup?
followups.a: Control Tower provisions accounts and baseline guardrails via its own automation (Account Factory). Terraform typically consumes the output (account IDs, VPC layouts, IAM paths) via data sources or SSM parameters rather than managing CT resources itself, since managing Control Tower resources in Terraform can fight CT's own guardrails. The boundary is: Control Tower owns account vending and baseline; Terraform owns workload resources within each account.

q: When does it make sense to build a custom Terraform provider, and what is the development and distribution lifecycle?
a: Write a custom provider when you need Terraform to manage an internal platform resource — a service catalog entry, an internal DNS record, a data-mesh asset registration, a cost-allocation tag policy — that no existing provider covers. The Terraform Plugin Framework (Go SDK) is the modern path; it uses a structured provider/resource schema, handles drift detection via Read, and integrates with `terraform-plugin-testing` for acceptance tests. Development lifecycle: scaffold with `tfplugingen-openapi` if the target has an OpenAPI spec, implement Create/Read/Update/Delete methods, write unit and acceptance tests that hit a real or mocked API endpoint, then publish to a private registry via a CI-released GitHub release with the binary attached. OpenTofu users can use the same plugin protocol. Key design pitfalls: forgetting to implement Read (causing phantom diffs), storing secrets in state unnecessarily, and not handling 404-means-deleted gracefully in Read so Terraform knows to recreate rather than error.
fs: Custom providers are justified for internal platform APIs without public providers; use Terraform Plugin Framework, implement full CRUD+Read, and publish to your private registry from CI.
fj: Use null_resource + local-exec to call any API from Terraform instead of writing a provider.

followups.q: Why is the Read method critical, and what happens if it always returns the cached state?
followups.a: Read is Terraform's drift-detection hook — it fetches current live attributes and updates state. If Read just returns cached values, Terraform never detects out-of-band changes, so drift is invisible and plans always show no changes even when reality diverged. A correct Read must call the real API and return the live state, setting `resp.State.RemoveResource()` on a 404 so Terraform plans to recreate the deleted object.

followups.q: How do you test a custom provider against a real API in CI without hitting production?
followups.a: Acceptance tests use `resource.Test` with `TF_ACC=1` and an environment that points to a test or sandbox instance of the target API. Stub the API with a mock server (e.g. using WireMock or a Go `httptest` server) for unit tests, and run full acceptance tests against an ephemeral dev environment in CI. Gate acceptance tests separately from unit tests due to runtime cost, and clean up provider-created resources after each test run.

q: How do you architect a drift-at-scale detection and remediation system for thousands of Terraform-managed resources?
a: Drift detection at scale requires scheduled, non-destructive plan runs across all root modules, aggregated into a centralized signal. The architecture: (1) scheduled CI jobs (cron) run `terraform plan -refresh-only` against every root module on a rolling basis and export plan JSON; (2) a drift-analysis service parses plan JSON for non-empty resource_changes and emits structured events per changed attribute — resource address, attribute name, old value, new value — to a data store (DynamoDB, BigQuery); (3) dashboards surface drift rate by team, resource type, and age; (4) remediation paths: auto-apply for pre-approved resource types (e.g. tag drift) using plan-then-apply automation, and alert + PR for structural or security-sensitive drift; (5) prevent drift at source using SCPs and preventive controls, since detection alone doesn't reduce toil. Tooling options: Driftctl (now Cloudyali-maintained), Firefly, or Terrateam's drift detection features can replace the homegrown scheduler if budget permits.
fs: Scheduled refresh-only plans across all roots, plan JSON parsed centrally for structured drift events, tiered remediation (auto-apply safe, PR-gated sensitive). Prevention SCPs reduce the signal.
fj: Drift is rare enough that we only check during on-call incidents.

followups.q: How do you avoid the scheduler's plan runs contending on state locks for large multi-region deployments?
followups.a: Shard the scheduler by state file (one job per root module) so plans on independent roots run concurrently without lock contention. Use `-refresh-only` which acquires the lock only briefly for the refresh read. For roots with very long plan times, parallelize with separate backend keys per region. If lock contention is still high, read-replica state backends (e.g. S3 with read replicas) can serve refresh reads without blocking writes.

followups.q: When should drift be auto-remediated versus requiring a human-reviewed PR?
followups.a: Auto-remediate low-blast-radius, reversible drift: tag additions, cost-allocation metadata, minor attribute defaults that the provider rounds after creation. Require human review for security-group rules, IAM policies, encryption settings, instance types, and anything that involves a forced replacement. The classification should be explicit policy — encoded in the drift-analysis service, not an informal judgment — and audited for false positives regularly.

q: How do you set org-wide IaC standards so 50 teams can move fast without creating a patchwork of incompatible Terraform patterns?
a: Org-wide IaC standards live at three layers: (1) Foundation — a golden-path module catalog with mandatory interfaces for compute, networking, data, and identity, backed by Terratest-validated releases; teams consume modules rather than raw provider resources, so the platform team encodes compliance, cost, and security defaults once; (2) Policy-as-code — Sentinel or OPA policies in CI that block anti-patterns and emit actionable messages with the compliant alternative; policies are versioned and tested like code, not a wiki page; (3) Scaffolding and developer experience — a Cookiecutter or Backstage template generates a new service's Terraform root with the correct backend, provider versions, module sources, and CI pipeline wired, so the zero-state starting point is already compliant. The governance body is a small IaC Guild (staff+ engineers from 3-4 domain teams + platform) that owns the standards RFC process, reviews major module proposals, and rotates breaking-change decisions quarterly. Standards should be enforced mechanically wherever possible and documented-but-optional where judgment is genuinely needed, to avoid a veto culture that slows delivery.
fs: Golden-path modules + policy-as-code enforcement + cookiecutter scaffolding = org standards that are enforced mechanically, not via wiki pages. Governance by IaC Guild, not a single gatekeeper.
fj: Post standards in Confluence and trust teams to follow them; tooling enforcement is overkill.

followups.q: How do you measure whether org-wide IaC standards are actually being adopted versus just existing on paper?
followups.a: Track leading indicators in the drift-analysis pipeline: count of raw provider resources outside approved module namespaces (policy violation rate), module version lag (how many roots are >1 major behind the registry), and time-to-green on a new service scaffold (DX metric). Combine with a quarterly 'IaC health score' per team derived from policy pass rate, lock file freshness, and test coverage on custom modules. Surface via a shared dashboard, not just a periodic audit.

followups.q: How do you handle a team that has critical legacy Terraform config that pre-dates all org standards?
followups.a: Don't force a big-bang migration — that stalls delivery and creates resentment. Negotiate a migration plan with the team's tech lead: new resources added to the legacy config must use approved modules immediately; existing resources get a time-boxed migration backlog (e.g. 2 quarters). Grant a policy exemption scoped to the specific resource addresses and set an expiry date in the exemption metadata. Track exemption burndown as a platform KPI. If the team is blocked by a missing module, the platform team owns unblocking them, not just enforcing the rule.

q: OpenTofu vs Terraform: how do you make and defend an org-level licensing and toolchain bet in 2025–2026?
a: The decision framework has three axes: legal risk, ecosystem fit, and differentiation value. Legal: Terraform's BSL prohibits building a competing product but is permissive for internal use — get a legal sign-off on your specific use case rather than assuming either way. OpenTofu is MPL-2.0 (OSI-approved), which removes that ambiguity entirely for any org building tooling or SaaS on top of IaC. Ecosystem: HCP Terraform/Enterprise and major ISVs (Spacelift, Env0) support both; the provider ecosystem is identical. Feature divergence is real: OpenTofu 1.7–1.9 shipped native state encryption, provider-defined functions, `-exclude`, early variable evaluation, and improved `for_each` on provider blocks, all not available in open-source Terraform. Terraform remains the stronger choice if you're buying HCP Terraform Business/Plus for run orchestration, SSO, and audit, since HCP integration is Terraform-native. The defensible bet in 2026: greenfield platforms and open-source-heavy shops should default to OpenTofu; enterprises with existing HCP Terraform contracts should stay on Terraform and revisit at contract renewal. Critically, don't mix tools in the same codebase — the divergence on state format and feature flags will eventually bite you.
fs: Legal clarity → MPL OpenTofu wins; HCP Terraform integration → Terraform wins; feature velocity → OpenTofu is ahead for open-source. Commit to one per codebase, don't mix.
fj: They're functionally identical so just pick whichever and move on.

followups.q: What concrete technical signal would make you recommend switching a Terraform org to OpenTofu mid-flight?
followups.a: Triggering signals: legal counsel flags BSL as a compliance risk for your SaaS product; you need client-side state encryption for a regulated workload and can't use HCP Terraform; you're blocked on a feature (e.g. provider `for_each`) that OpenTofu has shipped but Terraform hasn't, and the timeline is unclear. Migration is low-friction today (swap the binary, run `tofu init`), but requires auditing all modules for HCP-Terraform-specific features and verifying the lock file cross-platform. Execute in a non-production root first and validate state round-trips before committing org-wide.

followups.q: How do you communicate a toolchain migration decision to 50 teams without creating a support avalanche?
followups.a: Use the golden-path scaffold as the forcing function: update the Cookiecutter template and CI pipeline to use OpenTofu for new services, so all net-new work defaults to it without a conversation. Existing services stay on Terraform until their next major refactor. Publish a single migration runbook (binary swap + lock file regen + CI step update) and a migration FAQ. Designate 2-3 migration champions per domain group who've already done it and can field Slack questions. Track adoption with a dashboard metric, not periodic all-hands nagging.

q: How do you govern IaC practices across many autonomous teams so standards don't become bottlenecks?
a: The core tension is that centralized enforcement slows teams and distributed autonomy produces a patchwork. The resolution is 'paved roads, not guardrails': make the compliant path the easiest path so teams choose it, not because they're forced. The governance model that scales: (1) a small IaC platform team owns the golden-path module catalog, CI scaffold, and policy engine — they're not approvers, they're service providers; (2) policies are tiered: hard-block policies are narrow (security non-negotiables like unencrypted storage, public-by-default), soft-warn policies are broad (cost sizing, tagging completeness), advisory policies are opt-in guidance; (3) teams have a clearly documented override process — file an exception RFC, get platform + security sign-off, and the exception is tracked with a sunset date in policy metadata, not a spreadsheet; (4) inner-source the module catalog: any team can propose and own a module subject to platform review, which scales contribution and reduces the platform team bottleneck; (5) measure governance health via policy violation rate trend, not individual incident count, and publish it to leadership monthly. The anti-pattern is a central 'Terraform review board' that manually approves every change — this creates a single-threaded bottleneck and incentivizes teams to work around IaC entirely.
fs: Paved roads > guardrails: make compliance the easiest path, tier policies by severity, inner-source the module catalog, and track adoption via metrics not audits.
fj: Strong governance means the platform team approves every Terraform PR to catch mistakes early.

followups.q: How do you avoid the module catalog becoming a bottleneck when the platform team is small?
followups.a: Inner-source: any team can author and own a module, with the platform team acting as a reviewer rather than sole author. Define contribution criteria (must pass Terratest, must have auto-generated docs, must follow interface conventions) so review is criteria-based not judgment-based. Automate as much review as possible (linting, doc checks, test pass) so human review focuses only on interface design and security. Set an SLA of 2 business days for initial review feedback to remove the 'it gets ignored' perception.

followups.q: What leading metrics tell you that your IaC governance model is working before an incident exposes it?
followups.a: Track: policy hard-block violation rate (should trend toward zero, not oscillate), module version lag distribution (what % of roots are within 1 major of latest), time-from-PR-to-apply (governance shouldn't inflate this), exception backlog age (stale exceptions are a signal of governance debt), and 'raw resource leak rate' (resources created outside approved modules). If you only measure after incidents, you're governing reactively. Monthly governance review with these metrics surfaced to engineering leadership creates the accountability loop without manual policing.
